content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The river is fascinating. The bucket answers the question.RxJS teaches you to think in rivers. Every change becomes part of the current — queries, results, clicks, loading states. You build elegant pipelines to guide the flow.
Time flows. Our apps flow with it.
Until one day you realize you weren’t there to study the river.
You just wanted to know whether your bucket was full.
Everything has a value, even while it changes over time.
A user searches for “apple.” Results appear. A minute later, they click one.
Nothing new happened.
You don’t need another pipeline or withLatestFrom. You simply need the current value.
// RxJS
click$.pipe(withLatestFrom(searchResults$), ...)// Streamix
onClick(() => submitDetail(searchResults.current));Not every question is “What just happened?”
Many are simply “What is true right now?”
Most business logic isn’t about time. It’s about state.
Reactive-by-default assumes every change deserves attention.
Read-on-demand assumes attention should be intentional.
That’s the quiet heresy.
Not every changing value is a river.
Sometimes it’s simply a fact, waiting to be read.
The real win isn’t less code.
It’s mental bandwidth. When your tools stop whispering “make it reactive” in your ear, you finally have space to ask better questions — questions that belong to the domain, not the paradigm.
The next version of Streamix will be released in about a month.
But its most important change isn’t the API.
It’s the shift in perspective.
I’m writing the Developer Chronicles while that way of thinking is still evolving. If it resonates with you — or if you think it’s completely wrong — join the conversation.
After all, the best ideas rarely come from standing on only one bank of the river.
The real shift already happened: software that feels like a solution, not a demonstration of how cleverly we tamed the river.
